macro_segments.title: A Minor Girl's Kiddushin Without Father's Knowledge: Initial Cases and the Father's Absence

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Kiddushin of a Minor with Father's Knowledge, Father Leaves Country, Subsequent Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge
Debate Between Rav and Rav Assi: Presumption of Father's Approval When Father is Absent
Rav's Concession on Yerusha: Money Stays with Original Owner in Doubt
Reconciliation: Terumah is Miderabbanan, Yerusha is Biblical

macro_segments.title: A Minor Girl's Kiddushin When Father is Present and Silent: Interpreting Paternal Silence

micro_segments.title: First Case: Kiddushin with Father's Knowledge, Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge, Father Present
Dispute Between Rav Huna and Rav Yirmeya bar Abba on Silence
Second Case: Both Kiddushin and Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge, Father Present and Silent
Rava's Resolution: Pattern of Behavior Determines Interpretation

macro_segments.title: Explicit Objection and Acceptance: When Father Responds Directly to Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: Rav's Position: Both Father and Daughter Can Prevent or Accept Kiddushin
Tosafot's Clarification: Silence vs. Explicit Objection
Daughter's Miun: Limited Objection Rights and Conditions

macro_segments.title: Biblical Case of Seduction: Whether the Act Constitutes Kiddushin and Who Can Object

micro_segments.title: Rav Assi's Question: Deriving from Seduction Case Whether Daughter Can Refuse
Rav's Distinction: Seduction Not for Marriage vs. Seduction for Marriage
Rav Ashi's Question: If Seduction is Not for Marriage, Why Require Second Kiddushin?
Alternative Reading: Seduction for Marriage Requires Father's Approval
Tosafot's Explanation: Daughter's Objection and Payment of Fine
Problem with Daughter's Refusal Power: Father Remains Authority

macro_segments.title: Adult Woman's Kiddushin with Multiple Objects: Combining and Separating Acts

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Separate Acts (Hiskadshi, Hiskadshi) Require Each Object Worth a Peruta
Mishnah: Combined Statement (U'Zo U'Zo) Allows Objects to Combine to a Peruta
Mishnah: If She Eats Dates as Given, One Must Be Worth a Peruta Individually
Rav and Shmuel: First Case Applies (Separate Statements)
Rabbi Ami's Answer: Last Case Applies, Last Object Must Have Peruta
Rava's Three Inferences: Milveh Status, Mixed Objects, Money Returns
Concept of Milveh: Money Given for Kiddushin That Doesn't Take Effect

macro_segments.title: Invalid Kiddushin and Ownership Status: Marriage with Sister and Gift vs. Loan

micro_segments.title: Case: Marrying One's Sister; Dispute on Whether Ring is Gift or Returns
Rav's View: Money Returns; Understood as Pikadon (Deposit)
Shmuel's View: Money is Gift; Understood as Matanah to Avoid Embarrassment

macro_segments.title: Gift vs. Loan in Religious Context: Comparison with Terumah and Challah Cases

micro_segments.title: Ravina's Parallel: Separating Challah While Still Flour (Invalid); Does Gift vs. Loan Apply?
Concern: If Treated as Gift, Kohen May Think It's Really Challah
Comparison: Terumah from Plants Without Pots (Hydroponic Case)
Distinction: Two Vessels vs. One Vessel in Understanding
Conclusion: Flour Case Requires Return; Hydroponic Case Allows Keeping with Re-separation
